ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for packaged grass jelly drink; mapped to broad beverage food product class as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for grass jelly / ice jelly RTD beverages; A0CKM (non-alcoholic RTD beverages) is the closest defensible broad match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for packaged ice jelly drink; left null to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve packaged ice jelly drink is approximately 300 ml (300 g), providing about 180 kcal and 33 g of sugar per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Ice Jelly Drink (Packaged) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for packaged ice jelly drink (cr_id D05010026). All core macros and secondary nutrients are as reported. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, potassium, vitamins, and other micronutrients not reported by HPB for this entry; set to -1 per Veritas numeric hygiene rules.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged ice jelly drink. GI estimated at 62 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: sugar-sweetened soft drink / sweetened beverage (GI ~59–65) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The beverage is predominantly water and dissolved sugar (11.04 g/100 g) with jelly pieces providing negligible starch; the GI is driven by sucrose and glucose content. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (62 × 33.1 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 20.5 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (11.04 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk regardless of low sodium (10 mg/100 g), consistent with AHA 2018 guidance on SSBs and hypertension.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened packaged beverage; the 11 g/100 g sugar load independently elevates blood pressure risk and a 300 ml serving delivers approximately 33 g of sugar, far exceeding recommended daily added-sugar limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 11.04 g sugar/100 g and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar is absorbed rapidly, causing acute postprandial glucose spikes with no fibre buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely; a single 300 ml serving delivers approximately 33 g of rapidly absorbed sugar with no fibre, producing a glycemic load of approximately 20.5 and a sharp postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is independently associated with increased cardiovascular disease risk through triglyceride elevation, insulin resistance, and systemic inflammation; this product contains 11.04 g sugar/100 g with zero protective nutrients.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should eliminate sugar-sweetened packaged beverages such as this from their diet, as habitual intake raises triglycerides and promotes cardiometabolic risk independent of total caloric intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined sugar at 11.04 g/100 g with zero fibre, omega-3, polyphenols, or anti-inflammatory nutrients; promotes systemic inflammatory mediators relevant to RA disease activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage; its high refined sugar content promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity and it provides no anti-inflammatory nutrients such as omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenols, or dietary fibre.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged ice jelly drink is a ready-to-drink sweetened beverage popular across Singapore and Southeast Asia, sold in bottles or cans typically containing approximately 300 ml per serving. The drink consists of water, sugar, and small chewy pieces of grass jelly (xiancao in Chinese, cincau in Malay), which is derived from the dried stalks and leaves of Platostoma palustre, a plant in the mint family traditionally used in Chinese herbal medicine. The jelly pieces have a mild, slightly bitter herbal flavour and a soft, gelatinous texture that contrasts with the sweet liquid base. Per 100 g, the product provides 60 kcal, 15 g of carbohydrate (of which 11.04 g is sugar), and negligible protein and fat. Sodium is low at 10 mg/100 g. Dietary fibre is reported as zero. The beverage is classified as ultra-processed and is a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) by nutritional definition. Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high free-sugar content, rapid glycemic absorption profile (estimated GI 62, medium), and complete absence of protective nutrients such as fibre, omega-3 fatty acids, or polyphenols. A 300 ml serving delivers approximately 33 g of sugar and a glycemic load of approximately 20.5. The product is vegetarian, vegan, and typically halal-certified. It is commonly consumed as a refreshment drink in hawker centres, convenience stores, and supermarkets throughout Singapore and Malaysia.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05010026, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), clearly documented. Multilingual names are well-established for this widely consumed regional product.
notes: Zero-macro justification: protein_g is confirmed zero by HPB source — this is a water-and-sugar-based beverage with no protein-containing ingredients by formulation. fat_g is confirmed zero by HPB source — no fat-containing ingredients are present in this product formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source; grass jelly pieces in packaged RTD format contribute negligible fibre at commercial dilution levels. Negative sentinel values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this packaged beverage are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for D05010026 as of 2026. GI note: GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on sugar-sweetened beverages from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found in approved sources. Glycemic load calculated as (62 × 33.12 g carbohydrate per 300 g serving) / 100 = 20.5 GL units. Saturated fat reported as 0.0 consistent with zero total fat; this is a confirmed zero by formulation.
